rationale_for_baseline_tier: The underlying diagnosis — a flare of known Crohn's disease with associated peripheral enteropathic arthritis and a negative acute-red-flag screen — is on its own merits an outpatient-managed chronic flare (T2). The tier is lifted to T3 (urgent, same-day) on REAL patient-obtainable acuity, not on a reflexive can't-examine argument: the patient has mild clinical dehydration with inability to maintain safe oral hydration, is bedbound and cannot toilet safely, lives alone with a high fall risk, and has a missed biologic dose. Those functional/hydration factors require an urgent same-day face-to-face review (GP/urgent care/community rapid-response) for fluids, parenteral therapy and hands-on joint assessment. It is deliberately NOT T4/T5: there is no surgical abdomen (soft, passing diarrhoea and flatus, no vomiting), no septic-joint pattern (symmetrical, afebrile) and no shock (normal BP, SpO2, only mild tachycardia). Over-triage to ED/000 is the primary scored defect.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep ANY fluids down, worsening dizziness/presyncope on sitting or standing, or markedly reduced urine output
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day / immediately if fluids cannot be maintained
escalation_edges.rationale: Progression from mild to significant dehydration in an isolated, immobile patient warrants urgent in-person/emergency assessment for IV rehydration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep any fluids down at all, feel faint when you sit up, or you're passing very little urine, call for urgent help or go to the emergency department — you may need a drip.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: One joint becomes fiery red, hot and unbearably painful with a rising fever or shaking chills
escalation_edges.rationale: A single dominant hot joint with fever raises septic arthritis, which is a surgical emergency requiring urgent aspiration — a change from the current symmetrical afebrile pattern.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one joint turns bright red, very hot and unbearable and you spike a fever or get shaking chills, get seen urgently the same day — that joint may need to be tested for infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Abdomen becomes rigid/board-like, flatus and stool stop, or persistent/faeculent vomiting begins
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the signs of a true surgical abdomen/obstruction or peritonitis that were screened negative tonight — if they develop it becomes an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your stomach becomes hard like a board, you stop passing wind and stool, or you start vomiting repeatedly and can't stop, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fall, inability to get up, or feeling too unsafe/unsupported to remain at home
escalation_edges.rationale: The patient lives alone and is bedbound with a high fall risk; a fall or worsening inability to cope alone requires urgent in-person support even short of a medical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you fall, can't get yourself up, or feel unsafe on your own, call for urgent help so someone can come to you today.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present, bedbound tonight, cannot reach the toilet, high fall risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lifts urgency toward same-day face-to-face review and mandates arranging practical home/social support and fall-risk mitigation — a remote-only plan is unsafe for this patient tonight regardless of the benign diagnosis.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Established Crohn's disease on biologic therapy with a recently missed dose
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frames the presentation as a recognised chronic flare (avoiding over-triage) while making prompt gastroenterology review and biologic reinstatement the priority, and flagging NSAID avoidance and corticosteroid+biologic infection risk.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of your known bowel condition with the associated joint swelling, made worse by the missed injection — not a joint infection, a blocked bowel or an emergency. Because you are dehydrated, can't move safely and are on your own, you need to be seen in person today for fluids and treatment and to have the joints checked hands-on, and you need an urgent gastroenterology and GP review within 48 hours to restart your injection. Keep sipping fluids as best you can, use paracetamol (not ibuprofen or other anti-inflammatories) for pain, and arrange for someone to be with you or help you to the bathroom safely.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange urgent same-day GP / urgent-care or community rapid-response review; call an ambulance (000) only if the emergency red flags below develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, contact the after-hours GP or urgent-care service for a same-day review; use the emergency department or 000 only if the emergency triggers occur.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP and gastroenterologist with a flare summary: concurrent bowel and large-joint flare in known Crohn's, missed biologic dose, mild dehydration, negative acute-red-flag screen (no surgical abdomen, no septic-joint pattern), and the plan for rehydration, corticosteroid, hands-on joint assessment and urgent biologic reinstatement.
